content.text: PACT Contracts Between Claude Service Consumers

Consumer-Driven Contract (CDC) testing solves a specific problem: multiple teams consume a shared Claude service wrapper, and changes to that wrapper can break consumers without any end-to-end test catching it first. PACT is the most widely used CDC framework. Each consumer writes a "contract" — the exact HTTP interactions it depends on — and the provider verifies it can satisfy every contract before deploying.

attrs.alt: Consumer-Driven Contract flow for Claude services

content.text: Why CDC Testing Applies to Claude Services

A production Claude deployment is typically a thin internal service: it wraps the Anthropic API, adds auth, rate limiting, prompt templating, and logging. Multiple teams consume it with different assumptions — the chat team expects certain response shapes, the summary team expects different max_tokens defaults, the embed team calls a different endpoint entirely.

Without CDC testing, the Claude service team finds out about breaking changes from downstream incidents, not CI. Consumer contracts flip that: the provider learns about incompatibilities before deployment, not after.
Without CDC
With CDC
Breaking change caught in staging or prod
Breaking change caught in CI before deploy
Consumer teams file bugs after deploy
Provider CI fails on consumer contract verification
Root cause requires cross-team debug
Failing pact identifies exact interaction and consumer
Rollback required
Deploy blocked; fix made before release
Project Layout

services/
  claude-service/           # Provider
    app.py                  # FastAPI app wrapping Anthropic
    tests/
      pact/
        verify_pacts.py     # Provider verification test
  chat-frontend/            # Consumer A
    tests/
      pact/
        test_claude_pact.py
  summary-service/          # Consumer B
    tests/
      pact/
        test_claude_pact.py
pacts/                      # Generated pact JSON files (git-tracked)
Writing a Consumer Pact

The consumer test uses pact-python to mock the provider, drives the consumer code against the mock, and records the resulting contract as a JSON file. The contract describes: the request the consumer sends and the minimum response shape it needs.
pip install pact-python pytest

# chat-frontend/tests/pact/test_claude_pact.py
import pytest
import requests
from pact import Consumer, Provider

PACT_DIR = "pacts"
PACT_HOST = "localhost"
PACT_PORT = 8080

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def pact():
    p = Consumer("chat-frontend").has_pact_with(
        Provider("claude-service"),
        host_name=PACT_HOST,
        port=PACT_PORT,
        pact_dir=PACT_DIR,
        log_dir="/tmp/pact-logs",
    )
    p.start_service()
    yield p
    p.stop_service()

def test_chat_completion(pact):
    expected_response = {
        "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Paris is the capital of France."}],
        "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        "stop_reason": "end_turn",
    }
    (
        pact
        .given("the claude service is available")
        .upon_receiving("a chat completion request")
        .with_request(
            method="POST",
            path="/v1/messages",
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body={
                "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                "max_tokens": 256,
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
            },
        )
        .will_respond_with(
            status=200,
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body=expected_response,
        )
    )

    with pact:
        # Drive the actual consumer code against the pact mock
        resp = requests.post(
            f"http://{PACT_HOST}:{PACT_PORT}/v1/messages",
            json={
                "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                "max_tokens": 256,
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
            },
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        )
        assert resp.status_code == 200
        body = resp.json()
        assert body["content"][0]["text"] == "Paris is the capital of France."
Pact Matchers for Flexible Contracts
Hard-coding exact response values makes contracts brittle — the actual LLM response will differ from the mock. Use pact matchers to express structural constraints, not exact values.
The Generated Pact File

Running the consumer test generates a JSON pact file. This file is the contract — the machine-readable artifact the provider uses for verification. Commit it to the repo or publish it to the PACT broker.
Provider Verification

The Claude service (provider) fetches all consumer pacts from the broker or local directory and verifies each interaction against the live service. It runs a local server, replays each request, and checks the response matches the contract.

# claude-service/app.py  (simplified FastAPI wrapper)
import anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()
_client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class MessageRequest(BaseModel):
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    messages: list[dict]
    system: str = ""

@app.post("/v1/messages")
def create_message(req: MessageRequest):
    try:
        resp = _client.messages.create(
            model=req.model,
            max_tokens=req.max_tokens,
            messages=req.messages,
            system=req.system or anthropic.NOT_GIVEN,
        )
    except anthropic.APIError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=502, detail=str(exc)) from exc
    return {
        "content": [{"type": b.type, "text": b.text} for b in resp.content],
        "model": resp.model,
        "stop_reason": resp.stop_reason,
        "usage": {"input_tokens": resp.usage.input_tokens, "output_tokens": resp.usage.output_tokens},
    }

# claude-service/tests/pact/verify_pacts.py
import pytest
import uvicorn
import threading
from pact import Verifier
from app import app  # the FastAPI app

PROVIDER_PORT = 9090
PACT_DIR = "../../pacts"  # relative to this file

def start_provider():
    """Start the provider server in a background thread."""
    config = uvicorn.Config(app, host="127.0.0.1", port=PROVIDER_PORT, log_level="error")
    server = uvicorn.Server(config)
    thread = threading.Thread(target=server.run, daemon=True)
    thread.start()
    import time; time.sleep(1)  # wait for startup

def test_provider_pacts():
    start_provider()
    verifier = Verifier(
        provider="claude-service",
        provider_base_url=f"http://127.0.0.1:{PROVIDER_PORT}",
    )
    output, _ = verifier.verify_pacts(
        PACT_DIR,
        provider_states_setup_url=f"http://127.0.0.1:{PROVIDER_PORT}/_pact/provider-states",
    )
    # Non-zero output means at least one pact failed verification
    assert output == 0, "One or more consumer pacts failed provider verification"
Provider State Setup Endpoint
Provider states (the "given" clause in consumer tests) may require seeding data or mocking dependencies. The provider exposes a setup endpoint that the verifier calls before each interaction.

# claude-service/app.py  (provider state hook — add to same FastAPI app)
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
import os

# Only mount in test/verification mode
if os.environ.get("PACT_PROVIDER_VERIFICATION"):
    from fastapi import Request

    @app.post("/_pact/provider-states")
    async def provider_states(request: Request):
        body = await request.json()
        state = body.get("state", "")
        if state == "the claude service is available":
            # Patch Anthropic to return a deterministic canned response
            mock_resp = MagicMock()
            mock_resp.content = [MagicMock(type="text", text="Paris is the capital of France.")]
            mock_resp.model = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
            mock_resp.stop_reason = "end_turn"
            mock_resp.usage = MagicMock(input_tokens=10, output_tokens=8)
            # Store mock on app state so /v1/messages can pick it up
            app.state.mock_response = mock_resp
        return {"result": f"State '{state}' set up"}
Publishing Pacts to a Broker

The PACT broker is the central store that decouples consumer and provider CI pipelines. Consumers push pacts after their test suite passes; providers pull all pacts during their verification step. The broker also powers the can-i-deploy API.

# After consumer tests pass, publish pacts to broker
# pact-python includes the pact-broker CLI via pact-python[broker]
pip install "pact-python[broker]"

pact-broker publish pacts/ \
  --broker-base-url https://pact.mycompany.com \
  --broker-token "$PACT_BROKER_TOKEN" \
  --consumer-app-version "$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)" \
  --branch "$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)"

# Before deploying the provider, check can-i-deploy
pact-broker can-i-deploy \
  --broker-base-url https://pact.mycompany.com \
  --broker-token "$PACT_BROKER_TOKEN" \
  --pacticipant claude-service \
  --version "$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)" \
  --to-environment production

# Exit code 0 = all consumer pacts verified → safe to deploy
# Exit code 1 = one or more pacts unverified → block deployment
CI Integration

# .github/workflows/pact-consumer.yml
name: Consumer Pact Tests

on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]

jobs:
  pact-consumer:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install pact-python pytest requests
      - name: Run pact consumer tests
        working-directory: chat-frontend
        run: pytest tests/pact/ -v
      - name: Publish pacts to broker
        run: |
          pact-broker publish pacts/ \
            --broker-base-url ${{ vars.PACT_BROKER_URL }} \
            --broker-token ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }} \
            --consumer-app-version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --branch ${{ github.ref_name }}

# .github/workflows/pact-provider.yml
name: Provider Pact Verification

on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]
  # Also triggered when consumer publishes a new pact (via broker webhook)
  repository_dispatch:
    types: [pact-changed]

jobs:
  pact-provider:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      PACT_PROVIDER_VERIFICATION: "true"
      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install pact-python pytest uvicorn fastapi anthropic
      - name: Verify all consumer pacts
        working-directory: claude-service
        run: pytest tests/pact/verify_pacts.py -v
      - name: Can-I-Deploy check
        run: |
          pact-broker can-i-deploy \
            --broker-base-url ${{ vars.PACT_BROKER_URL }} \
            --broker-token ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }} \
            --pacticipant claude-service \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --to-environment production
What to Contract-Test vs What to Skip
Include in PACT
Skip — Use Other Tests Instead
HTTP request/response shape for each consumer
LLM output quality (use evals)
Error response shapes (4xx, 5xx)
Prompt engineering effectiveness (use evals)
Auth header presence and format
Load and latency (use performance tests)
Required fields in request body
Exact text content of AI responses
Content-Type negotiation
Internal implementation details of the wrapper

Pact contracts verify interface compatibility — that the provider speaks the language the consumer expects. They do not verify that the AI model produces correct or useful output. That is the job of eval suites.
Keeping Contracts Up to Date
Run consumer tests on every PR — new interactions auto-add to the pact file.
Version pacts by git SHA or semantic version so providers can verify against specific consumer releases.
Use broker webhooks to trigger provider verification whenever a consumer publishes a new pact.
Delete interactions from the pact when the consumer no longer calls that endpoint — stale interactions waste verification time.
Never hand-edit the generated pact JSON — re-run consumer tests to regenerate.
Summary

PACT consumer-driven contracts give Claude service teams a safety net against breaking changes. Consumers write tests against a mock provider and publish the resulting contract. The provider CI fetches and verifies all contracts before deploying. The PACT broker's can-i-deploy API enforces the gate: no deploy until every consumer's pact passes verification. This eliminates the category of cross-service integration failures that are invisible to unit tests and evals.
